title: Moist Heat Cooking Methods
content:
MOIST HEAT METHODS

Boiling: Cooking in liquid at 212°F (sea level). Used primarily for pasta, some vegetables, and initial cooking steps. Avoid prolonged boiling as it toughens proteins and destroys nutrients.

Simmering: Cooking in liquid at 140-185°F, just below boiling. Bubbles form gently beneath surface. Ideal for soups, stews, braises, and delicate foods.

Poaching: Cooking in liquid just under boiling point with constant basting or self-basting. Use poaching paper for meats and fish to allow steam escape while maintaining moisture.

Steaming: Cooking in steam over boiling water. Excellent for vegetables, fish, and puddings. Keeps nutrients intact better than boiling.

Braising: Searing meat in hot fat, then simmering in small amount of liquid until tender. Combines dry and moist heat.

Fricasseeing: Searing meat in hot fat, then simmering in small amount of water until tender. Similar to braising but with less fat.

Stewing: Cooking food in liquid that barely covers it, at simmering temperature. Long, slow cooking tenderizes tough cuts.

Casseroling: Cooking in a covered casserole dish in moderate oven (300°F). Self-basting occurs as condensation forms on lid. Deglaze residue for sauce.

Pressure Cooking: Cooking by steam under pressure at 250°F (at 15 lbs pressure). Cooks in one-third the time of conventional methods. Never fill more than half full with liquid or two-thirds with solids. Reduce pressure immediately after cooking time (except for puddings and some meats).
